title: Cirrhosis — Compensated
oneLiner: Chronic liver disease with preserved hepatic function; no ascites, jaundice, variceal bleeding, or encephalopathy. Risk of decompensation, HCC, and death if untreated.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Variceal haemorrhage** — can present suddenly even in “compensated” patients.  
  *Single finding:* any melaena, haematemesis, or unexplained hypotension/tachycardia (check stool colour, rectal exam).
- **Acute-on-chronic liver failure (ACLF)** — e.g. triggered by infection, alcohol binge, or NSAIDs.  
  *Single finding:* new confusion (asterixis) + rising bilirubin/INR in a patient with known cirrhosis.
- **Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP)** — subtle even in well-compensated patients; look for *new or worsening ascites* (not yet present in compensated stage, but can be the first sign of decompensation).  
  *Single finding:* fever + abdominal pain + ascites (but ascites may be minimal; diagnostic paracentesis is key).
sections.Presentation:
- Often **asymptomatic** — incidental finding on imaging or bloods (low platelets, prolonged INR, raised bilirubin).
- **Non-specific symptoms:** fatigue, anorexia, weight loss, pruritus, muscle cramps.
- **Signs of chronic liver disease** (not all present):
  - Spider naevi, palmar erythema, gynaecomastia (male), clubbing, leuconychia.
  - Small irregular liver (shrunken) or enlarged (if steatohepatitis).
  - Splenomegaly (portal hypertension).
  - Caput medusae, fluid thrill (ascites – if present, now decompensated).
- Remember: **compensated** = no ascites, no jaundice, no hepatic encephalopathy, no variceal bleeding. If you see these, the patient has decompensated cirrhosis.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
1. **Steatohepatitis (NASH/ASH) without cirrhosis** — likely if no portal hypertension signs; liver stiffness <12.5 kPa (FibroScan).
2. **Chronic hepatitis B/C with fibrosis** — positive viral serology, higher transaminases.
3. **Autoimmune hepatitis** — raised IgG, anti-smooth muscle antibody, liver biopsy interface hepatitis.
4. **Primary biliary cholangitis** — pruritus + anti-mitochondrial antibody + raised ALP.
5. **Haemochromatosis** — high ferritin, transferrin saturation >45%, *HFE* gene mutation.
- **Splitter:** low platelets + splenomegaly on imaging = cirrhosis rather than fibrosis alone. Check FibroScan or transient elastography to differentiate.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine dipstick (exclude UTI – can precipitate decompensation).
  - Ascitic tap only if fluid present (then send for neutrophil count, culture, albumin).
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (low platelets), coagulation profile (INR), LFTs (ALT/AST/ALP/GGT/bilirubin), U&Es, Cr.
  - **Fibrosis score** (e.g. APRI, FIB-4) to confirm cirrhosis if not already known.
  - Viral screen: HBsAg, anti-HCV, anti-HAV IgG (vaccinate if negative).
  - Iron studies, ferritin, transferrin saturation; caeruloplasmin (young <40); autoimmune screen (ANA, anti-SMA, anti-LKM1); α1-antitrypsin.
  - AFP (to screen for HCC).
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Abdominal ultrasound with Doppler (assess liver echotexture, splenomegaly, portal vein patency, HCC surveillance every 6 months).
  - **Second-line:** CT liver triphasic or MRI if ultrasound suspicious for HCC; FibroScan if fibrosis score needed.
- **Special tests:**
  - Upper GI endoscopy (OGD) at diagnosis to screen for varices – repeat frequency per NICE (if no varices, 3-yearly; if small varices, yearly).
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Child-Pugh score (A=5–6 = compensated, B=7–9, C=10–15) – determine class A. MELD score for transplant listing.
- **Never-miss test:** **Variceal screening OGD** – omission is a serious error; variceal bleeding is preventable.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Rule out decompensation:** check for jaundice, ascites, confusion, bleeding signs. If present, escalate and treat accordingly (e.g. IV terlipressin + antibiotics for variceal bleed).
  - If stable, **confirm diagnosis** with bloods + ultrasound ± FibroScan.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Cause-specific therapy:**
    - Alcohol-related: offer alcohol cessation support (e.g. benzodiazepine withdrawal protocol if dependent). Refer to addiction services.
    - NAFLD/NASH: lifestyle modification, weight loss, treat diabetes/dyslipidaemia.
    - HBV/HCV: refer to hepatology for antiviral therapy.
    - Autoimmune: consider steroids after biopsy.
  - **Surveillance:**
    - OGD within 2 weeks of diagnosis (if not done as outpatient).
    - Ultrasound + AFP every 6 months for HCC.
  - **Vaccination:** HAV, HBV, pneumococcal, influenza, COVID-19 (if not immune).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Prophylaxis for variceal bleeding:** if medium/large varices on OGD → **non-selective beta-blocker** (propranolol or carvedilol) or endoscopic band ligation (EBL). Start beta-blocker as outpatient, titrate to HR 55–60 bpm.
  - **Portal hypertension management:** avoid NSAIDs (increase bleeding risk); treat ascites if appears.
  - **Screening for HCC** – ultrasound ± AFP every 6 months (NICE CG131).
  - **Bone density** (DEXA if Child-Pugh B/C or malabsorption).
- **Default Orders (if admitted for any reason):**
  - No IV fluids unless dehydrated (use oral if possible; avoid excess sodium).
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (enoxaparin 40mg SC OD) **but** check platelets >50; avoid if INR >2.0.
  - Diet: normal, no alcohol. If ascites develops, salt restriction (<2g Na/day).
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly unless patient has other acute condition.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Propranolol** for variceal prophylaxis: start 40mg orally twice daily; titrate every 3 days to HR 55–60 bpm (max dose varies – refer to local protocol). [Check local policy if unsure about up-titration]
  - **Carvedilol** alternative: 6.25mg once daily, increase to 12.5mg once daily if tolerated.
  - **Vitamin B1 (thiamine)** if alcohol-related: 100mg IV/IM immediately, then 100mg PO daily (Pabrinex if severe withdrawal).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Spironolactone** (if ascites develops) – not first-line for compensated stage.
  - **Proton pump inhibitor** only if OGD shows variceal bleeding or gastropathy (avoid routine PPI – increases SBP risk).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid (bleeding risk, renal impairment).
  - β-blockers: contraindicated in asthma, heart block, decompensated acutely (e.g. active variceal bleed – use only after haemostasis).
  - **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** dose of beta-blockers unchanged in mild cirrhosis; avoid carvedilol in severe hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Propranolol/carvedilol** – starting too high or titrating too fast → bradycardia, hypotension, renal failure. Always check HR before each dose.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Variceal haemorrhage – present with haematemesis, melaena; treat with terlipressin + broad-spectrum antibiotics + urgent EBL.
  - Hepatic encephalopathy – triggered by infection, constipation, bleed; check ammonia (not essential), treat with lactulose 15–30mL BD (adjust to 2–3 soft stools/day).
- **Chronic:**
  - Progression to decompensated cirrhosis (ascites, jaundice, SBP, hepatorenal syndrome).
  - Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) – incidence ~3–4%/year.
  - Portal hypertensive gastropathy, osteopenia, malnutrition.
sections.Prognosis:
- **Child-Pugh A (compensated):** median survival >10 years if underlying cause controlled. 5-year risk of decompensation ~20–30% if untreated.
- Without therapy (e.g. ongoing alcohol use, viral replication), rapid progression to decompensation and death.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **DO** offer variceal screening OGD within 2 weeks of diagnosis (even if well).
- **DO** screen for HCC every 6 months – ultrasound + AFP (NICE CG131).
- **DO** vaccinate against HAV, HBV, pneumococcus, influenza.
- **DO** refer to hepatology if any uncertainty or if patient develops decompensation.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **NOT** prescribing beta-blocker for variceal prophylaxis without OGD results (may cause harm if no varices).
- **NOT** using NSAIDs for pain (ibuprofen, diclofenac) – risk of GI bleeding and acute kidney injury.
- **NOT** ignoring subtle decompensation – e.g., mild jaundice, trace ascites, or confusion – check INR, bilirubin, Cr, and consider paracentesis.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Any new or worsening jaundice (yellow skin/eyes).
  - Abdominal swelling (ascites) or weight gain >2kg in 3 days.
  - Dark/black stools or vomiting blood.
  - Confusion, drowsiness, or personality change (hepatic encephalopathy).
  - Fever >38°C, especially with abdominal pain (SBP).
- **Follow-up:**
  - Hepatology outpatient within 4 weeks (or 2 weeks if OGD pending).
  - HCC surveillance appointment (radiology/US) to be arranged before discharge.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior/reg immediately if:**
  - Any acute haemorrhage (MUST attend ED, call GI bleed on-call).
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2.0 mmol/L.
  - GCS drop <13 or new asterixis (hepatic encephalopathy – start lactulose, call hepatology).
  - Suspected SBP – urgent paracentesis + IV antibiotics (e.g. ceftriaxone 2g IV) after cultures.
- **ICU criteria:** need for vasopressors, respiratory support, continuous renal replacement therapy (e.g. hepatorenal syndrome).

rawMarkdown:
## Cirrhosis — Compensated  
**One-liner:** Chronic liver disease with preserved hepatic function; no ascites, jaundice, variceal bleeding, or encephalopathy. Risk of decompensation, HCC, and death if untreated.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Variceal haemorrhage** — can present suddenly even in “compensated” patients.  
  *Single finding:* any melaena, haematemesis, or unexplained hypotension/tachycardia (check stool colour, rectal exam).
- **Acute-on-chronic liver failure (ACLF)** — e.g. triggered by infection, alcohol binge, or NSAIDs.  
  *Single finding:* new confusion (asterixis) + rising bilirubin/INR in a patient with known cirrhosis.
- **Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis (SBP)** — subtle even in well-compensated patients; look for *new or worsening ascites* (not yet present in compensated stage, but can be the first sign of decompensation).  
  *Single finding:* fever + abdominal pain + ascites (but ascites may be minimal; diagnostic paracentesis is key).

### Presentation
- Often **asymptomatic** — incidental finding on imaging or bloods (low platelets, prolonged INR, raised bilirubin).
- **Non-specific symptoms:** fatigue, anorexia, weight loss, pruritus, muscle cramps.
- **Signs of chronic liver disease** (not all present):
  - Spider naevi, palmar erythema, gynaecomastia (male), clubbing, leuconychia.
  - Small irregular liver (shrunken) or enlarged (if steatohepatitis).
  - Splenomegaly (portal hypertension).
  - Caput medusae, fluid thrill (ascites – if present, now decompensated).
- Remember: **compensated** = no ascites, no jaundice, no hepatic encephalopathy, no variceal bleeding. If you see these, the patient has decompensated cirrhosis.

### Differential Diagnosis
1. **Steatohepatitis (NASH/ASH) without cirrhosis** — likely if no portal hypertension signs; liver stiffness <12.5 kPa (FibroScan).
2. **Chronic hepatitis B/C with fibrosis** — positive viral serology, higher transaminases.
3. **Autoimmune hepatitis** — raised IgG, anti-smooth muscle antibody, liver biopsy interface hepatitis.
4. **Primary biliary cholangitis** — pruritus + anti-mitochondrial antibody + raised ALP.
5. **Haemochromatosis** — high ferritin, transferrin saturation >45%, *HFE* gene mutation.
- **Splitter:** low platelets + splenomegaly on imaging = cirrhosis rather than fibrosis alone. Check FibroScan or transient elastography to differentiate.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Urine dipstick (exclude UTI – can precipitate decompensation).
  - Ascitic tap only if fluid present (then send for neutrophil count, culture, albumin).
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (low platelets), coagulation profile (INR), LFTs (ALT/AST/ALP/GGT/bilirubin), U&Es, Cr.
  - **Fibrosis score** (e.g. APRI, FIB-4) to confirm cirrhosis if not already known.
  - Viral screen: HBsAg, anti-HCV, anti-HAV IgG (vaccinate if negative).
  - Iron studies, ferritin, transferrin saturation; caeruloplasmin (young <40); autoimmune screen (ANA, anti-SMA, anti-LKM1); α1-antitrypsin.
  - AFP (to screen for HCC).
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Abdominal ultrasound with Doppler (assess liver echotexture, splenomegaly, portal vein patency, HCC surveillance every 6 months).
  - **Second-line:** CT liver triphasic or MRI if ultrasound suspicious for HCC; FibroScan if fibrosis score needed.
- **Special tests:**
  - Upper GI endoscopy (OGD) at diagnosis to screen for varices – repeat frequency per NICE (if no varices, 3-yearly; if small varices, yearly).
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Child-Pugh score (A=5–6 = compensated, B=7–9, C=10–15) – determine class A. MELD score for transplant listing.
- **Never-miss test:** **Variceal screening OGD** – omission is a serious error; variceal bleeding is preventable.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - **Rule out decompensation:** check for jaundice, ascites, confusion, bleeding signs. If present, escalate and treat accordingly (e.g. IV terlipressin + antibiotics for variceal bleed).
  - If stable, **confirm diagnosis** with bloods + ultrasound ± FibroScan.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - **Cause-specific therapy:**
    - Alcohol-related: offer alcohol cessation support (e.g. benzodiazepine withdrawal protocol if dependent). Refer to addiction services.
    - NAFLD/NASH: lifestyle modification, weight loss, treat diabetes/dyslipidaemia.
    - HBV/HCV: refer to hepatology for antiviral therapy.
    - Autoimmune: consider steroids after biopsy.
  - **Surveillance:**
    - OGD within 2 weeks of diagnosis (if not done as outpatient).
    - Ultrasound + AFP every 6 months for HCC.
  - **Vaccination:** HAV, HBV, pneumococcal, influenza, COVID-19 (if not immune).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Prophylaxis for variceal bleeding:** if medium/large varices on OGD → **non-selective beta-blocker** (propranolol or carvedilol) or endoscopic band ligation (EBL). Start beta-blocker as outpatient, titrate to HR 55–60 bpm.
  - **Portal hypertension management:** avoid NSAIDs (increase bleeding risk); treat ascites if appears.
  - **Screening for HCC** – ultrasound ± AFP every 6 months (NICE CG131).
  - **Bone density** (DEXA if Child-Pugh B/C or malabsorption).
- **Default Orders (if admitted for any reason):**
  - No IV fluids unless dehydrated (use oral if possible; avoid excess sodium).
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (enoxaparin 40mg SC OD) **but** check platelets >50; avoid if INR >2.0.
  - Diet: normal, no alcohol. If ascites develops, salt restriction (<2g Na/day).
  - Observations: NEWS2 4-hourly unless patient has other acute condition.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Propranolol** for variceal prophylaxis: start 40mg orally twice daily; titrate every 3 days to HR 55–60 bpm (max dose varies – refer to local protocol). [Check local policy if unsure about up-titration]
  - **Carvedilol** alternative: 6.25mg once daily, increase to 12.5mg once daily if tolerated.
  - **Vitamin B1 (thiamine)** if alcohol-related: 100mg IV/IM immediately, then 100mg PO daily (Pabrinex if severe withdrawal).
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Spironolactone** (if ascites develops) – not first-line for compensated stage.
  - **Proton pump inhibitor** only if OGD shows variceal bleeding or gastropathy (avoid routine PPI – increases SBP risk).
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - NSAIDs: avoid (bleeding risk, renal impairment).
  - β-blockers: contraindicated in asthma, heart block, decompensated acutely (e.g. active variceal bleed – use only after haemostasis).
  - **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** dose of beta-blockers unchanged in mild cirrhosis; avoid carvedilol in severe hepatic impairment.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Propranolol/carvedilol** – starting too high or titrating too fast → bradycardia, hypotension, renal failure. Always check HR before each dose.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Variceal haemorrhage – present with haematemesis, melaena; treat with terlipressin + broad-spectrum antibiotics + urgent EBL.
  - Hepatic encephalopathy – triggered by infection, constipation, bleed; check ammonia (not essential), treat with lactulose 15–30mL BD (adjust to 2–3 soft stools/day).
- **Chronic:**
  - Progression to decompensated cirrhosis (ascites, jaundice, SBP, hepatorenal syndrome).
  - Hepatocellular carcinoma (HCC) – incidence ~3–4%/year.
  - Portal hypertensive gastropathy, osteopenia, malnutrition.

### Prognosis
- **Child-Pugh A (compensated):** median survival >10 years if underlying cause controlled. 5-year risk of decompensation ~20–30% if untreated.
- Without therapy (e.g. ongoing alcohol use, viral replication), rapid progression to decompensation and death.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **DO** offer variceal screening OGD within 2 weeks of diagnosis (even if well).
- **DO** screen for HCC every 6 months – ultrasound + AFP (NICE CG131).
- **DO** vaccinate against HAV, HBV, pneumococcus, influenza.
- **DO** refer to hepatology if any uncertainty or if patient develops decompensation.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **NOT** prescribing beta-blocker for variceal prophylaxis without OGD results (may cause harm if no varices).
- **NOT** using NSAIDs for pain (ibuprofen, diclofenac) – risk of GI bleeding and acute kidney injury.
- **NOT** ignoring subtle decompensation – e.g., mild jaundice, trace ascites, or confusion – check INR, bilirubin, Cr, and consider paracentesis.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Any new or worsening jaundice (yellow skin/eyes).
  - Abdominal swelling (ascites) or weight gain >2kg in 3 days.
  - Dark/black stools or vomiting blood.
  - Confusion, drowsiness, or personality change (hepatic encephalopathy).
  - Fever >38°C, especially with abdominal pain (SBP).
- **Follow-up:**
  - Hepatology outpatient within 4 weeks (or 2 weeks if OGD pending).
  - HCC surveillance appointment (radiology/US) to be arranged before discharge.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior/reg immediately if:**
  - Any acute haemorrhage (MUST attend ED, call GI bleed on-call).
  - NEWS2 ≥5 or lactate >2.0 mmol/L.
  - GCS drop <13 or new asterixis (hepatic encephalopathy – start lactulose, call hepatology).
  - Suspected SBP – urgent paracentesis + IV antibiotics (e.g. ceftriaxone 2g IV) after cultures.
- **ICU criteria:** need for vasopressors, respiratory support, continuous renal replacement therapy (e.g. hepatorenal syndrome).
